data.title: Wall Street crowds into AI plays as ownership breadth jumps
data.description:
Bank of America’s global research team highlighted a sharp shift in institutional positioning, with artificial intelligence–linked names driving the largest gains in ownership breadth across the S&P 500 (SP500) over the past year.

The data, based on the percentage of funds holding each stock, underscores how investors have increasingly concentrated exposure in companies tied to AI infrastructure, semiconductors, and data ecosystems. 

Leading the trend is Palantir Technologies (PLTR), which saw ownership breadth jump by 24 percentage points year over year. Industrial player GE Vernova (GEV) followed with a 15-point increase, reflecting rising interest in electrification themes tied to AI demand. Semiconductor and hardware-related firms—including Broadcom (AVGO), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Arista Networks (ANET)—also ranked prominently. 

The full list of stocks by BofA showing the largest increase in ownership breadth includes Palantir Technologies (PLTR), GE Vernova (GEV), Amphenol Corp (APH), Western Digital (WDC), Broadcom (AVGO), Gilead Sciences (GILD), Meta Platforms (META), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Arista Networks (ANET), and McKesson Corp (MCK). 

Notably, while technology dominates the rankings, healthcare names like Gilead Sciences and McKesson Corp. also saw meaningful accumulation. Overall, the findings reflect a market increasingly tilted toward companies viewed as core beneficiaries of long-term AI adoption and digital infrastructure expansion. 

S&P 500 Funds: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (FXAIX), (VFIAX), (VFFSX), and (SWPPX).

data.title: Purple projects $500M-$520M 2026 revenue with $20M-$30M adjusted EBITDA, backed by Mattress Firm and Costco expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Purple Innovation (PRPL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Robert DeMartini (CEO & Director) said Q4 2025 was “an important inflection point,” citing “Revenue increased approximately 9% year-over-year,” “adjusted EBITDA of approximately $8.8 million,” and that Purple “finished the year profitable,” attributing the change to cost actions, “a full quarter of expanded Mattress Firm distribution and a significant expansion of our Costco program.”
  • DeMartini said the company is shifting priorities: “It marks a clear shift from defense to offense,” and described 2026 priorities as “deepening our understanding of the consumer, delivering better sleep through product experience and expanded distribution and executing with financial discipline across the business.”
  • DeMartini highlighted channel and product mix trends, including “Comparable sales in our showrooms increased 8.8%” and “Rejuvenate 2.0 represented over 50% of showroom mattress revenue during the quarter,” while also noting “E-commerce performance was mixed during the year and declined in the fourth quarter.”
  • DeMartini detailed wholesale/distribution expansion, including that Costco expanded to “approximately 450 clubs bringing us to nearly nationwide distribution,” and that Mattress Firm’s Purple Royale launch was “originally planned for over 2,800 slots bringing us to a total of 12,000 slots across Mattress Firm's 2,200 stores,” adding that “Mattress Firm is adding incremental slots as the product launches.”
  • Todd Vogensen (CFO & Treasurer) said, “Net revenue for the fourth quarter was $140.7 million,” and added, “Our fourth quarter adjusted loss per share was $0.02,” while “Adjusted EBITDA in the fourth quarter was $8.8 million.”
  • DeMartini said the company’s Form 10-K included “a going concern qualification,” adding, “we want to provide clear context why the decisive, transformative actions we've already taken are expected to continue stabilizing our financial position,” and later stated, “We expect to conclude Q1 '26...with neutral cash burn.”
  • DeMartini reiterated the strategic alternatives review: “The process remains ongoing,” and said the company “will not be commenting further or taking questions on this topic during today's Q&A.”

Outlook

  • Q1 2026 revenue guidance of $100M-$105M vs. analysts’ $120.730210M; Q1 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance “a loss of $7 million to a loss of $4 million.”
  • Full-year 2026 revenue guidance of $500M-$520M and adjusted EBITDA of $20M-$30M, which DeMartini said “reflects continued momentum in our premium product portfolio, expanded wholesale distribution and the operating leverage in the business as volume grows,” and added, “this guidance is driven by execution, not by a recovery in the broader market.”
  • Vogensen framed the year’s cadence around product and seasonality, saying Purple Royale “just got out on floors last week officially” (for Q2), with “a natural build...going into Q3 and Q4,” and that revenue should “build pretty consistently as we go across the course of the year.”
  • Compared with Q3 2025, management moved from reiterating 2025 guidance (“revenue in the range of $465 million to $485 million and adjusted EBITDA between breakeven and $10 million positive”) to issuing explicit 2026 ranges and adding Q1 2026 guidance.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 net revenue was $140.7M vs. analysts’ $139.807850M, and adjusted loss per share was $0.02 (no analysts EPS figure was provided in the transcript).
  • Vogensen said Q4 channel results were DTC net revenue of $71.9M (with showroom revenue up “approximately 4.5%” and e-commerce down “15.3%”) and wholesale revenue up “approximately 39.8%,” driven by “expanded Mattress Firm placements and continued momentum with Costco.”
  • Vogensen reported Q4 gross margin of “approximately 41.9%,” while noting it was “down 100 basis points from last year,” and tied the margin profile to “direct material savings, plant efficiencies, restructuring benefits and volume leverage.”
  • For full-year 2025, Vogensen reported net revenue of $468.7M, gross margin of 40.2%, operating expenses of $231.6M, adjusted EBITDA of $1.9M, and cash and cash equivalents of $24.3M at year-end.
  • Vogensen highlighted financing runway: “We also extended our debt maturities from December 31, 2026 to April 30, 2027,” which he said enhanced flexibility and reflected “continued strong support and confidence from our lending partners.”

Q&A

  • Bradley Thomas, KeyBanc: asked about a Q1 “step back” after Q4 momentum; Robert DeMartini (CEO & Director) said Q4 shipments “did impact demand in January,” cited Costco “buy-in in December,” and said Mattress Firm floor samples “has a short-term push down on revenue,” adding, “Q1 has always been our weakest quarter.”
  • Bradley Thomas, KeyBanc: asked about flow-through; Todd Vogensen (CFO & Treasurer) said, “flow-through actually should be quite good,” and added, “on a normal basis, our sales should be generating about a 30% flow-through.”
  • Bradley Thomas, KeyBanc: asked about raw-material exposure; Vogensen said petroleum-based inputs include “foam” and “the mineral oil that's going into the gel,” and added that if oil stays near “$100 a barrel,” tariff savings and mitigation would “roughly offset the exposure.”
  • Matt Koranda, ROTH: asked about seasonality and implied ramp; Vogensen said Purple Royale’s floor timing supports Q2 and the year should “build pretty consistently.” Matt Koranda, ROTH: asked if more restructuring is needed; Vogensen said, “We don't have plans for additional actions that are needed right now,” but referenced “a little bit of an action in January” that benefits operating expense.
  • Daniel Silverstein, UBS: asked why the $70M Mattress Firm opportunity doesn’t fully map to the $30M-$50M revenue growth implied by guidance; DeMartini said the $70M is something Purple must “grow into,” framed it as “somewhere between $50 million and $70 million,” and said the plan assumes “a flat e-commerce business...we tried to show some conservatism there.”
  • Daniel Silverstein, UBS: asked Amazon vs. Purple e-commerce; DeMartini said Purple’s own site faces reduced “specialness” as distribution expands, and that assortment can be “a neutral or a negative in a digital environment,” while Amazon gains relate to an “underdeveloped” business and “availability and prime badging.”
  • Alessandra Jimenez, Raymond James: asked about partner trends beyond Mattress Firm/Costco; DeMartini said it was “a mixed bag,” adding that backing out the two highlighted customers implies “a net down about 5%.” Alessandra Jimenez, Raymond James: asked about 2026 cash flow; Vogensen said, “we would expect positive free cash flow for the year.”
  • Brian Nagel, Oppenheimer: asked if innovative product rollout is complete and about capital needs; DeMartini said the Rejuvenate rollout was completed “in the middle of fourth quarter” and Royale officially launched March 20, with a “full innovation pipeline for other products starting in early '27,” while Vogensen said 2026 CapEx is “$10 million to $12 million.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative (analysts) as questions pressed on Q1 softness, the gap between Mattress Firm expansion expectations and total revenue guidance, and durability of e-commerce; Silverstein’s framing included, “what other areas might be driving a bit of a drag.”
  • Slightly positive (management) with confidence anchored to execution and structural changes; DeMartini said “the momentum is real,” and Vogensen said, “We feel like we're positioned very well for the full year.”
  • Compared with Q3 2025, management’s tone added urgency around liquidity optics and disclosures, explicitly addressing “a going concern qualification,” while continuing to emphasize structural margin improvements and distribution expansion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance posture shifted from Q3’s reiterated full-year 2025 ranges to Q4’s explicit full-year 2026 revenue and adjusted EBITDA ranges plus Q1 2026 guidance.
  • Strategic narrative broadened in Q4 from restructuring progress and margin stabilization to a declared pivot: “shift from defense to offense,” with more emphasis on scaling premium products through Mattress Firm and Costco, and on Amazon as a reach/assortment lever.
  • Q4 introduced new disclosure focus versus Q3, with DeMartini addressing the “going concern qualification” and highlighting debt maturity extension to April 2027.
  • Analyst focus remained consistent around demand cadence, flow-through, wholesale productivity, and e-commerce pressure; Q4 added more pointed questions about reconciling Mattress Firm expansion math to the full-year revenue outlook.

Risks and Concerns

  • DeMartini disclosed, “our independent auditor has included a going concern qualification,” and tied mitigation to restructuring results, lender support, and expectations for “neutral cash burn” in Q1 2026.
  • Management flagged a “challenging” broader market and e-commerce weakness, with DeMartini noting online “specialness...has been challenged” as distribution expands.
  • Vogensen discussed input-cost uncertainty, saying supplier “noises about price increases” were “very, very early on,” and linked potential oil-driven pressure to tariff mitigation.
  • DeMartini described variability across wholesale partners as “a mixed bag,” indicating uneven performance outside Mattress Firm and Costco.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q4 2025 as a profitability and margin inflection, anchored by wholesale acceleration from Mattress Firm and Costco, premium mix gains from Rejuvenate 2.0, and cost actions the company described as “structural.” For 2026, executives guided to $500M-$520M in revenue and $20M-$30M in adjusted EBITDA while also disclosing a 10-K “going concern qualification,” pointing to extended debt maturities to April 2027 and expectations for neutral Q1 cash burn as key elements of the stabilization narrative.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AI, chip stocks jump as Trump hints at ending Iran war within weeks
data.description:
Chip and AI-related stocks jumped on Tuesday following a report that U.S. President Donald Trump intends to wind down the military campaign against Iran within weeks.

The Wall Street Journal reported that Trump told aides he is willing to end hostilities on a four-to-six-week timeline, prioritizing the degradation of Iran’s navy and missile stocks over the more complex mission of reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Trump also urged countries hit by Strait of Hormuz fuel disruptions to buy from the U.S. or “take it” themselves in remarks highlighting tensions with allies over the Iran war.

Matt Gertken, BCA Research’s geopolitical and U.S. political strategist, says there is “zero chance” the U.S. will launch a conventional full-scale ground invasion of Iran, but the deep mistrust between the two nations means Tehran is clinging to its nuclear leverage more tightly than ever.

Brent Futures (CO1:COM) climbed about 0.5%, while Crude Oil Futures (CL1:COM) rose nearly 1.2%. The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) jumped about 1.8%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) rose about 1.3%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) climbed nearly 1.1%.

Shares of AI chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA) surged about 3%, while Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) rose nearly 2%. Broadcom (AVGO) jumped around 3, while Qualcomm (QCOM) was largely flat but in the green.

Several other AI and networking-related stocks were also among the gainers. Celestica (CLS) surged around 5%, Coherent (COHR) soared about 4%, and Ciena (CIEN) jumped nearly 3%. Arista Networks (ANET), Corning (GLW), and Lumentum (LITE) each rose about 2%. Cisco (CSCO) was also in the green. Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) seemed to buck the trend and dipped about 2%.

Marvell Technology (MRVL), in which Nvidia is planning a $2B investment, surged about 8%. Arm (ARM) and Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) soared around 5%, while GlobalFoundries (GFS) surged nearly 4%. Intel (INTC) and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) each jumped roughly 3%. Analog Devices (ADI) and Texas Instruments (TXN) each rose about 2%. Micron Technology (MU) was largely flat but in the green.

Earlier on Tuesday it was reported that Micron's rivals, Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and SK hynix (HXSCL), have four to six months worth of helium inventory, a gas ‌crucial for chipmaking. Shares of Samsung fell about 5%, while SK hynix tumbled nearly 8% in South Korea on Tuesday. Samsung and SK hynix are among the largest memory chipmaking companies in the world. SK hynix is a major supplier of high-bandwidth memory, or HBM, chips to Nvidia (NVDA). Samsung and Micron also compete with SK hynix in this space.

Chip equipment makers: ASML (ASML), Lam Research (LRCX) and KLA (KLAC) each soared about 3%. Applied Materials (AMAT) rose around 2%.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Spar Group outlines $143M-$151M fiscal 2026 revenue outlook with 20.5%-22.5% gross margin target, driven by merchandising mix shift
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SPAR Group, Inc. (SGRP) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO William Linnane framed fiscal 2025 as a strategic reset, saying, “Fiscal 2025 was a transformational year for SPAR. We finalized the work connected to the divestiture of our international joint ventures, a deliberate decision that allowed us to concentrate fully on growing our business in the U.S. and Canada.”

  • CEO Linnane described an operating model shift toward “intelligent outcome-based action,” stating, “SPAR is redefining retail execution around intelligent outcome-based action, a model where data, technology and in-store execution converge in real time on demand.”

  • CEO Linnane highlighted a new partnership intended to combine data signals with in-store execution, saying, “Last week, we announced a strategic partnership with ReposiTrak,” and later adding, “The ReposiTrak partnership adds the intelligence layer, out-of-stock detection, perpetual inventory accuracy and route optimization so that our dispatch decisions are data-driven, not reactive.”

  • CFO Steven Hennen summarized the year’s results and cost structure, including: “Fiscal 2025 net revenues totaled $136.1 million,” and “SG&A costs included approximately $7 million of onetime costs and out-of-period write-offs in 2025.”

Outlook

  • Management issued fiscal 2026 guidance, with CEO William Linnane stating, “We expect top line revenue to be in the range of $143 million to $151 million and gross margins to improve to 20.5% to 22.5%, primarily driven by our service mix with a growing percent of merchandising work relative to remodel work.”

  • CEO Linnane linked the 2026 setup to pipeline and share dynamics, saying the company is “encouraged by the growing strength of our business pipeline, driven by wallet expansion from existing clients and market share gains this year.”

  • Seasonality expectations were discussed at a high level, with CFO Steven Hennen saying, “When we provided the guidance that we released today, that is on an annual basis. Now the only quarter that we see kind of below that, potentially at the bottom end of that range is the fourth quarter, which is typically our slowest quarter of the year.”

  • No analysts’ estimates were provided in usable form in the prompt, so no comparison was included.

Financial Results

  • CFO Steven Hennen reported fiscal 2025 net revenues of $136.1 million, and on a recast comparable basis said “full year revenues of $136.1 million for the United States and Canada increased by 3.3% over 2024,” with “U.S. net revenues increased 3.9% to $122.1 million, while Canadian sales were essentially flat at $14.1 million.”

  • Profitability pressure was concentrated in margin and cost items, with CFO Hennen stating, “Our gross profit for the year was $21.7 million or 15.9% of revenue compared with $33.6 million or 20.5% of revenue in 2024,” adding that “gross margin compression in 2025 was primarily due to shift towards the remodeling business, which inherently carries higher labor and travel costs, market-driven wage pressure and shifts in workforce alignment.”

  • CFO Hennen detailed operating and net results, saying, “we reported operating loss of $16.9 million for the fiscal year 2025 compared to $700,000 of operating income in the prior fiscal period,” and “Net loss attributable to SPAR Group, Inc. for 2025 was $24.6 million or $1.04 per diluted share compared to a net loss of $3.2 million or $0.13 per share in 2024.”

  • CFO Hennen also provided adjusted and EBITDA metrics, including: “Adjusted net loss attributable to SPAR Group, Inc. was $10.7 million or $0.45 per diluted share,” and “Consolidated adjusted EBITDA was a negative $8.6 million compared to a positive $6.7 million in the prior year.”

  • Liquidity and cash flow items highlighted included CFO Hennen’s statement, “This includes $3.3 million in cash and cash equivalents,” and “net cash used by operating activities was $18.4 million.”

Q&A

  • Ross Davidson, Benetton Capital: asked for “color around both the revenue decline and, I guess, the resulting negative gross margin” in Q4 and how that transitions into 2026; President, CEO & Director William Linnane attributed it to project timing and mix, responding, “we had some timing of projects in terms of how they land in 2025 and how they land in 2024,” and added the shift is “related to the focus back on to really growing merchandising as opposed to remodel business,” which he later reinforced as a deliberate sales pivot “given the margin difference between the 2 businesses.”

  • Ross Davidson, Benetton Capital: asked about quarterly cadence and whether to expect a build toward the guided gross margin; Chief Financial Officer Steven Hennen emphasized the guidance is annual and said, “the only quarter that we see kind of below that, potentially at the bottom end of that range is the fourth quarter, which is typically our slowest quarter of the year,” while President, CEO & Director Linnane added that gross margin includes “field management costs, which is somewhat semi fixed,” and said, “we've intentionally pivoted strongly to focus back office merchandising.”

  • Ross Davidson, Benetton Capital: asked whether the ReposiTrak partnership is live and being marketed; President, CEO & Director Linnane replied, “That's correct. Meetings are actually in progress in terms of conversations. So yes, it's live,” and positioned it as “the first of potentially some other announcements we'll make into the future.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone centered on Q4 performance questions, with Ross Davidson, Benetton Capital pressing on “the resulting negative gross margin” and the “inflecting” into 2026.

  • Slightly positive management tone emphasized strategic momentum and forward plans, with President, CEO & Director William Linnane saying, “We are bullish about what this partnership and other similar partnerships unlock for SPAR in 2026 and beyond,” while also using confident execution language such as, “If we execute consistently, decisively and with discipline, SPAR will not just participate but will lead in the future of retail execution.”

  • No prior-quarter transcript was provided beyond “not available,” so tone shifts versus the prior call could not be assessed.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The prior-quarter transcript content was not available in the prompt, so management guidance changes, metric shifts, and Q&A focus differences versus the previous call were not identifiable.

Risks and Concerns

  • CFO Steven Hennen pointed to business-mix and cost pressures as drivers of fiscal 2025 margin compression, citing “shift towards the remodeling business,” “higher labor and travel costs,” and “market-driven wage pressure.”

  • CFO Hennen identified notable non-recurring and restructuring items, stating, “SG&A costs included approximately $7 million of onetime costs and out-of-period write-offs in 2025,” and “we recorded restructuring costs and severance of $4.8 million for the 2025 fiscal year-end,” while also giving a forward view on normalized overhead: “We expect our annual run rate SG&A costs to be approximately $25.5 million to $26.5 million, excluding any unusual and nonrecurring costs.”

  • CEO William Linnane emphasized execution risk and dependence on scaling the model, stating, “The work ahead is significant, but the direction is clear.”

Final Takeaway

Management positioned fiscal 2025 as a post-divestiture reset to a North America-focused model, highlighted a live partnership with ReposiTrak to pair out-of-stock detection and routing intelligence with “surge or on-demand merchandising,” and guided fiscal 2026 revenue of $143 million to $151 million with gross margin of 20.5% to 22.5% as the company targets a higher merchandising mix and a more normalized cost structure after $7 million of one-time SG&A items and $4.8 million of restructuring and severance costs in fiscal 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Solesence targets 30% gross margin floor in 2026 as it aims for 5% gross margin lift under Transform and Transcend
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Solesence, Inc. (SLSN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Kevin Cureton framed 2026 as an execution-focused year, saying the company will provide “more guidance on our 2026 plan and the strategy we initiated at the end of 2025,” calling the program “Transform and Transcend.”
  • CFO Laura Riffner reported Q4 revenue of $12.5 million and gross profit of $3.4 million, with gross margin at 27%, and said results “were affected by transition costs and operational inefficiencies in manufacturing resulting from our facility consolidation.”
  • CFO Riffner said Q4 operating expenses were $3.2 million, including “relocation charges as we transitioned from 3 facilities to 2,” and the quarter included net income of $163,000.
  • CFO Riffner said full-year 2025 revenue was a record $62.1 million, driven by “a large-scale launch in the first half of 2025 as well as 20 new brand partners who launched products in 2025,” while gross profit was $16.1 million.
  • CEO Cureton highlighted scale and IP, saying the company “increased our patent portfolio by 20%, which now numbers over 120,” and that capacity investments “will enable us to generate over $200 million in revenue without further major investment.”
  • CEO Cureton described an innovation push around “Day Mode Hero Concealer,” calling it a hybrid product leveraging “2 new technology platforms that we will bring to market in 2026,” and said it was named a finalist in four Cosmetics & Toiletries Alle Awards categories.
  • CEO Cureton said Transform and Transcend targets the same pressure points flagged previously: “product design, labor efficiency and inventory control,” and described four pillars including “operational excellence,” “technology-driven expansion,” a “shift toward a product development and supply model” emphasizing turnkey supply and co-marketing, and “collaborative globalization” starting in Q1 2027.
  • CEO Cureton detailed leadership changes, stating, “The change in leadership, starting with my appointment as President and Chief Executive Officer, was made to achieve profitable growth,” adding the company hired CFO Laura Riffner in September 2025 and “recently added Yoolie Park as Vice President of Brand Partnerships.”

Outlook

  • CFO Laura Riffner set 2026 profitability guardrails: “we are establishing a 30% gross margin floor as our target for the year,” and added, “We expect EBITDA improvement in 2026, returning to double digits as we realize 6-figure annual savings from our facility consolidation and the elimination of 2025's operational inefficiencies.”
  • CFO Riffner said free cash flow is a “critical goal” via working-capital actions: “increase our free cash flow by reducing safety stock and improving procurement operations.”
  • CFO Riffner flagged near-term pressures: “our first quarter results will be impacted by investments in training and restructuring… as well as by changes in customer order patterns… and weak sell-through from one of our large mass market customers.”
  • CFO Riffner cited softer demand indicators, saying “ship in open orders stand at just under $33 million as compared to a year ago when they were at $38 million,” while adding, “we anticipate a period of revenue normalization.”
  • On guidance specificity, CFO Riffner told an investor asking about EPS, “we aren't prepared to provide guidance on that this morning,” while reiterating the EBITDA target and saying gross margin guidance is “intentionally conservative.”

Financial Results

  • CFO Laura Riffner reported Q4 2025 revenue of $12.5 million, gross profit of $3.4 million, gross margin of 27%, operating expenses of $3.2 million, and net income of $163,000.
  • CFO Riffner attributed Q4 cost pressure to operational transitions, citing “transition costs and operational inefficiencies in manufacturing resulting from our facility consolidation.”
  • For full-year 2025, CFO Riffner reported revenue of $62.1 million and gross profit of $16.1 million, and said margin compression came from “labor costs,” “product design” costs tied to “a complex launch,” and “inventory control,” where the company “experienced yield volatility and associated losses.”
  • CFO Riffner reported full-year adjusted EBITDA of $4.2 million, describing it as “less than 7% of revenue.”

Q&A

  • Tony Rubin, investor: asked whether “EPS in 2026 be at or above the 2024 levels” and challenged the shift from “mid-30s” gross margins to a “floor of 30%.” Chief Financial Officer Laura Riffner: “we aren't prepared to provide guidance on that this morning,” and said the gross margin floor is “intentionally conservative” with an “intention… to leverage the Transform and Transcend initiative to improve that number.” President, CEO & Director Kevin Cureton: said the company is “taking a conservative approach” and is focused on “improving enterprise value.”
  • James Lieberman, American Trust Investment Services: asked for a road map to $200 million revenue capability and “an aspirational profit margin… as high as like a 40%.” President, CEO & Director Cureton: said consolidation was successful with “no… negative impact on OTIF,” and said initiatives should “significantly increase our gross profit margin performance… targeting levels that you mentioned and maybe even greater,” while emphasizing it “takes time.”
  • Stefano Bolis, investor: asked if the company still planned a dedicated investor call and asked about “BASF volumes” declining. President, CEO & Director Cureton: said it is the company’s “intent to provide improved communications,” referencing an upcoming “investor presentation,” but added on BASF that he would “not be able to provide more than that” regarding specific partner performance.
  • Wayne Rowan, investor: questioned why the company “struggled so much on production,” whether it “lost a brand partner,” why the call timing was later, and whether sales will improve. President, CEO & Director Cureton: attributed production issues to simultaneously growing and “installed new capability,” while prioritizing “quality standards… and… on-time and in full,” and said, “We did not mention… any loss of any brand partner,” clarifying the issue was “sell-through in the mass market” for one partner. Chief Financial Officer Riffner: said the call was timed after “our year-end audit is completed.” CEO Cureton: reiterated 2026 as “a period of normalization.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts/investors: slightly negative to mixed, with pointed challenges on profitability visibility and execution, including Tony Rubin saying, “I heard a lot of interesting words,” and pressing for EPS and margin specifics; Stefano Bolis pressed on BASF volume trends; Wayne Rowan pushed on “struggled so much on production.”
  • Management: slightly positive but cautious, repeatedly emphasizing conservatism and process, with Laura Riffner stating the “guidance is intentionally conservative,” and Kevin Cureton emphasizing confidence in plans while limiting disclosure on specific customers and EPS guidance.
  • Compared with the prior quarter, the current call carried more explicit conservatism on near-term demand (“revenue normalization,” lower open orders) and more structured strategic framing via Transform and Transcend; the prior quarter’s tone leaned more toward operational fixes underway and margin recovery expectations.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from Q3’s margin ambition toward Q4’s formalized guardrails, with Q4 introducing a “30% gross margin floor” for 2026, while Q3 management discussed the goal that “30% is really the floor… not the ceiling” and also referenced an objective to reach “40-plus-percent” gross margins over time.
  • Demand signals changed materially in the order metric management cited, with Q4 “ship in open orders” at “just under $33 million” vs. Q3’s “shipment open orders… $64 million,” reflecting different definitions/timeframes as described by management across calls.
  • Strategic communication advanced from Q3’s discussion of operational problem areas (product design, labor, inventory) into Q4’s formal rollout of Transform and Transcend with four pillars and timelines, including market expansion in late Q2 to early Q3 2026 and globalization beginning in Q1 2027.
  • Analyst focus remained consistent quarter-to-quarter on operational execution, margins, and forecasting clarity; Q4 added sharper scrutiny on EPS guidance and the perceived step-down in margin framing.

Risks and Concerns

  • CFO Laura Riffner warned Q1 2026 will be pressured by “investments in training and restructuring” and “changes in customer order patterns,” including “weak sell-through from one of our large mass market customers.”
  • Management repeatedly cited operational risk from scaling, with CEO Kevin Cureton pointing to constraints revealed by rapid growth and the need to correct “product design, labor efficiency and inventory control.”
  • On customer concentration/disclosure risk, CEO Cureton said he could not provide “specific guidance” on BASF or other partners’ performance.

Final Takeaway

Management positioned 2026 as an execution reset under Transform and Transcend, pairing a 30% gross margin floor target with plans for double-digit EBITDA and improved free cash flow via reduced safety stock and procurement improvements, while acknowledging near-term headwinds from restructuring investments and shifting customer order patterns tied to weak sell-through at a large mass-market customer. Leadership emphasized operational fixes (lean/SIOP, yield and inventory controls) alongside planned category expansion in late Q2 to early Q3 2026 and international support starting Q1 2027, while investors pressed for clearer EPS targets and faster margin recovery.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Subprime borrowers are having trouble paying loans, widening the wealth gap in America – Moody’s Zandi
data.description:
Mark Zandi, chief economist at Moody’s Analytics, is sounding the alarm on a troubling economic indicator: subprime borrowers are increasingly struggling to keep up with their loan payments.

As of February, more than 10% of debt owed by borrowers with credit scores below 660 at origination was delinquent, which Zandi noted is the “highest delinquency rate in more than a decade.”

 

 

 

Despite the concerning trend, the economist said he is “not particularly worried about what this means for the financial system.”

According to Zandi, subprime debt levels are significantly lower than before the Global Financial Crisis, when $3.5T in subprime debt represented about 30% of total debt outstanding. Today, subprime debt stands at $2.7T, accounting for only 15% of total outstanding debt.

What worries Zandi more is what these numbers reveal about the financial health of lower- and middle-income Americans. Even with an unemployment rate of just 4.4%, these borrowers are struggling to make their payments while facing $4-per-gallon gas prices—up a dollar from a month ago—along with higher interest rates and little prospect for refinancing their debts.

“I shudder to think what will happen if unemployment continues to rise,” he said.

The strategist emphasized that how subprime borrowers are faring serves as a “reliable leading economic indicator, signaling troubled times” ahead.

These warning signs emerged even before the economic fallout from hostilities in the Middle East, suggesting the financial strain on everyday Americans could intensify in the months ahead, he added.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Job openings continue to dwindle in February: JOLTS report
  • Consumer confidence unexpectedly strengthens in March
  • Chicago PMI falls more than expected in March
  • Home-price growth decelerates in January: S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Home Price Index
  • FHFA house price index rises modestly in January

data.title: Aehr jumps after receiving order from new customer for data center market
data.description:
• Aehr Test Systems (AEHR) rose more than 13% on Tuesday after the semiconductor testing firm announced it had received an order from a “major new customer” for the data center market. 
  • The Fremont, Calif.-based Aehr said it had received the order from the unnamed customer, who is developing advanced silicon photonics-based transceivers for data center networking and optical I/O applications. Aehr will provide the customer with its FOX-XP wafer-level burn-in system, a fully integrated WaferPak Auto Aligner, multiple FOX-NP wafer-level burn-in systems, and multiple FOX WaferPak full-wafer contactors.
  • Aehr added that the customer has also provided a forecast for additional systems as soon as this calendar year.
  • “Data center architectures are rapidly evolving as AI infrastructure scales, driving a major transition from traditional copper interconnects to fiber optic communication links to meet the increasing demands for bandwidth, latency, signal integrity, thermal performance, and power efficiency,” said Aehr CEO and President Gayn Erickson in a statement. “Silicon photonics-based optical transceivers are a critical enabling technology for this transformation, and we believe this order is particularly significant because it comes from a major new customer that is a leading supplier of networking products and solutions for the data center market.”

data.title: Norwegian's conservative guidance shouldn't spook investors -- Stifel
data.description:
After three days in the red, weighed down by the volatility in the energy markets and the fluid geopolitical climate, shares of Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH) are grinding higher, buoyed by a retreat in oil prices and an endorsement from Stifel.

In a note to clients after the company’s investor event, Stifel analyst Steven Wieczynski believes investors are incorrectly assuming management is “talking down yield guidance for the remainder of 2026."

“In our view, NOTHING (and we can’t say this loud enough) has changed versus when we met with [CEO John Chidsey] in mid-March,” Wieczynski says, noting that the company withheld yield guidance for the second half of this year to emphasize that Q3 will be the toughest from a yield perspective, with most of their inventory in weaker European and Alaskan markets.

“It absolutely makes sense for NCLH to be conservative [with FY26 guidance]. With a new CEO plus an activist on the scene, why not just write off 2026 and give the company time to focus on 2027, as nothing really can be done at this point in 2026 given the elongated booking window of the cruise industry?” Wieczynski says.

At issue is the company’s 2026 guidance  and comments surrounding pressure on pricing and yields. During the earnings call with analysts, CFO Mark Kempa noted that Norwegian entered 2026 slightly behind an ideal booking curve in certain itineraries, “creating near-term pressure on pricing and yield, which is evidenced in guidance.” Accordingly, Norwegian now expects its net yield to contract ~1.6% as higher occupancy is offset by pricing pressures. For the balance of the year, net yields are expected to stabilize and “modestly improve” by 0.6%, bringing full-year net yields to approximately flat.

As this conservatism likely spooked investors, Wieczynski believes this fueled a “significant dislocation” in the share price/valuation and that investors are overlooking two important factors in regard to NCLH. First, the company has adequate liquidity to de-lever the balance sheet and move closer to investment grade. And second, the core cruise customer/cruise industry has proven resilient during shocks to the system. 

“All told, we expect customer demand to again prove resilient…which in turn should allow the company to be in a steady state of meaningful profitability under most scenarios. Through that lens, we encourage investors to take advantage of the current market dislocation to accumulate shares for the long term,” Wieczynski advises.

Stifel maintains a Buy rating on Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH) and a $28 price target, which represents a 59% upside to Monday’s closing price.

data.title: Regeneron a new overweight at Piper on Dupixent, pipeline
data.description:
Piper Sandler has initiated Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) at overweight saying that it expects continued growth in Dupixent (dupilumab) sales as well as "several high-potential pipeline assets."

The firm set a price target of $875 (~17% upside based on March 30 close).

Analyst Biren Amin is projecting Dupixent sales to surge ~50% from 2025-2030, reaching about ~$20B by the end of that period.

He noted that the company has done well to ameliorate competition from standard-dose Eylea (aflibercept) with Eylea HD, as the latter now accounts for 47% of the franchise's sales in the US. Amin added that loss of sales to biosimilars should be lessened by an anticipated Q2 approval of a prefilled syringe ("could serve as the next catalyst for REGN shares") and the recent approval of monthly dosing.

Amin called Regeneron's pipeline "underappreciated," emphasizing its Factor XI antibody program. He models peak risk-adjusted sales of (REGN7508/REGN9933) of $2.7B compared to the consensus of $1.1B. "By decoupling efficacy from bleeding risk, this next-generation anticoagulation approach has the potential to unlock the ~50% of the market currently untreated due to safety concerns."

Elsewhere in the pipeline, other potential long-term revenue drivers are "fianlimab in metastatic melanoma, cemidisiran in immunology, and cat/birch allergy programs."

data.title: Snap gains on report activist Irenic has taken a stake
data.description:
Snap Inc. (SNAP) jumped 8.5% on a report that activist investor Irenic Capital Management has taken a stake.

Irenic, which has an economic interest of ~2.5% in Snap's Class A shares, wants the company to spin off or shutter its smart glasses business, Specs, according to a Bloomberg report on Tuesday, which cited a letter Irenic sent to Snap Chief Executive Officer and co-founder Evan Spiegel. The activist believes that moves Snap could make could boost its shares to more than $26 each.

The activist fund also wants Snap (SNAP) to rationalize its cost structure by cutting jobs  and changing its compensation structure for employees, according to the report. Irenic also is pushing Snap to concentrate on adopting artificial intelligence to improve ad monetization. Irenic also recommends a stock buyback with the shares so discounted. Snap shares have dropped more than 50% over the past year.

Michael Lynton, chairperson of Snap’s board responded to the report on Irenic to Bloomberg, saying the company has “taken steps to improve performance, strengthen free cash flow, and offset dilution, and will continue to evaluate actions that drive long-term value for all stockholders.”
